_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: MS/ MD/ DNB/DGO or Equivalent (Obstetrics & Gynecology)
clinical_indications: Suspicion of Asherman's syndrome in patients investigated for secondary amenorrhea or oligomenorrhoea with or without infertility

preauth.label: Detailed Clinical notes with history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings and advice for admission

ppd_preauth.q: Detailed Clinical notes – all vitals, detailed history, symptoms, signs, physical examination including local examination, indication for procedure, planned line of treatment and advice for admission?
Did history and imaging confirm the diagnosis?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Hysteroscopy findings not indicative of surgery
Detailed Clinical notes missing or incomplete (vitals, history, symptoms, signs, examination findings, indication, planned treatment, advice for admission)
History and imaging did not confirm diagnosis
Detailed ICPs missing or lacking daily vitals and treatment details
Discharge summary missing or lacking follow-up advice

additional_information.special_empanelment: Facilities with well-equipped operation theatre, anesthesia and anesthetist availability
clinical_key_pointers: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis is a blunt or sharp dissection using mechanical, electrosurgical instruments, Versapoint bipolar electrode or laser methods.
Different techniques have been described depending on the severity of the intrauterine synechia.
Hysteroscopy is the optimum route for treatment of Asherman's syndrome.
Asherman's syndrome: This condition refers to the presence of fibrosis and adhesions inside the cervical canal or the uterine cavity.
Intrauterine synechiae or adhesions are caused by trauma to the endometrium.
More than one session of operative hysteroscopy may be required to treat moderate to severe cases.
Recurrence is higher in those with more significant adhesions initially.
Treatment technique is largely influenced by the nature, extent and site of the intrauterine injury.
In most cases, menstruation improves following treatment.

Regarding fertility restoration – pregnancy rate of 50% in treated patients has been reported but with increased complication rate including pregnancy loss and abnormal placentation (especially placenta accreta).
